topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper seemed to vibrate under my gaze. A C-minus. It was an insult, a blatant miscalculation of my effort. I snatched the graded essay from the desk, the cheap paper crinkling loudly in my grip. My jaw tightened until my molars ached. I slammed the paper down onto the cluttered wooden surface, the sound sharp and final in the quiet library corner. "This is ridiculous," I muttered, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. I paced three steps, then spun back, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the table. Every word I’d poured onto that page, every late night spent wrestling with the concepts, reduced to this paltry mark. I felt a hot flush creep up my neck, a tightening sensation in my chest that made breathing shallow. I shoved my textbooks back into their worn satchel with unnecessary force, the leather groaning in protest. I needed to rip something, anything, just to release the pressure building behind my eyes.

[story 2]

I stared at the grade displayed on the screen, the numbers mocking me with their mediocrity. It wasn't just a score; it was a complete dismissal of the hours I’d sacrificed. My hands clenched into fists, the tension radiating up my forearms. I let out a slow, ragged exhale that sounded more like a hiss than a breath. "Unbelievable," I whispered, the sound brittle and low. I stood up so abruptly my chair scraped backward across the linoleum floor, a jarring noise in the otherwise hushed hallway. My gaze swept over the empty classroom, landing on the professor’s office door, which seemed miles away and utterly inaccessible. A deep, simmering heat bloomed in my stomach, a restless churning that demanded immediate release. I walked toward the exit, my steps heavy and deliberate, each footfall a declaration of utter dissatisfaction with the outcome.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, a cold, hard knot formed instantly beneath my ribs. I didn't even look at the actual score immediately; I just stared at the screen, my breathing becoming shallow and quick. A low growl started deep in my throat, a vibration I could feel more than hear. I slowly lowered my hand, the movement stiff, as if moving through thick water. I picked up the paper, turning it over and over, tracing the poorly marked sections with a fingertip. My entire body felt coiled, vibrating with a furious energy that refused to settle. "They don't see it," I said, my voice dangerously flat, devoid of any warmth. I dropped the paper onto the floor with a deliberate thud, the sound echoing in the small room. I began to pace in a tight circle, my shoulders hunched high toward my ears, a physical manifestation of the sheer frustration boiling just beneath the surface.
